titleStmt.title: SII 1.75: original edition by Eugen Hultzsch
PART II. TAMIL AND GRANTHA INSCRIPTIONS. IV. INSCRIPTIONS AT TIRUMALAI NEAR POLŇ™R. No. 75. ON THE OUTER WALL OF THE DOORWAY, WHICH LEADS TO THE PAINTED CAVE AT TIRUMALAI.

summary.p: This inscription is much obliterated. It consists of a passage in Tamil prose, a Sanskrit verse in the ŇöńĀrdŇęla metre, and another Tamil prose passage, and records some gifts made by VyńĀmukta-ŇõravaŠĻáojjvala or (in Tamil) ViŠłću-kńĀdaŠĻõagiya-PerumńĀŠł∑, alias AtigaimńĀŠĻČ[i]This might be meant for Ati-igai-mńĀŠĻČi, ‚Äúhe whose pride consists in excessive liberality.‚ÄĚ of the Chera race. The name of the capital of this prince seems to have been TakaŠĻ≠ńĀ. He was the son of some RńĀjarńĀja and a descendant of a certain YavanikńĀ, king of KeraŠł∑a, or (in Tamil) EŠĻõiŠĻČi, king of Va√Īji.Va√Īji or KaruvŇęr was the old capital of the Chera kingdom. Ptolemy calls it , ‚ÄúKarŇęr, the capital of the prince of KeraŠł∑a;‚ÄĚ see Dr. Caldwell's Comparative Grammar, 2nd edition, page 96 of the Introduction; Dr. Burnell's South-Indian Palaeography, 2nd edition, page 33, note 2; and Dr. B√ľhler in the Zeitschrift d. Deutsch. Morg. Ges. Vol. XXXVII, p. 99. The king repaired the images of a yaksha and a yakshńę, which had been made by YavanikńĀ, placed them on the Tirumalai Hill, presented a gong and constructed a channel. The Tirumalai Hill is here called Arhasugiri (the excellent mountain of the Arha[t]) and (in Tamil) EŠĻáguŠĻáaviŠĻüai-Tirumalai (the holy mountain of the Arhat). According to the Sanskrit portion of the inscription, it belonged to the TuŠĻáŠłćńęra-maŠĻáŠłćala; this seems to be a Sanskritised form of the well-known ToŠĻáŠłćai-maŠĻáŠłćalam.In a Western Chalukya inscription, ToŠĻáŠłćai-maŠĻáŠłćalam is called TuŠĻáŠłćńĀka-vishaya; see the fac-simile in Ind. Ant. Vol. VIII, p. 26, plate iiib, line 1. ToŠĻáŠłćai-maŠĻáŠłćalam itself occurs in No. 81.

revisionDesc.change: Further conversion of digital encoding to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)

div.p:
ceravaŠĻÖ¬∑Ňõattu AtikaimńĀŠĻČi EŠłĽiŠĻČi ceyta dharmma
yakŠĻ£araiyum yakŠĻ£iyńĀraiyum EŠłĽuntaruŠł∑uvittu EŠĻüimaŠĻáiyum I
ŠĻ≠ŠĻ≠ukkaŠĻ≠apperikkńĀluŠĻÖkaŠĻáŠĻ≠u kuŠĻ≠uttńĀŠĻČ .

lg.l:
ŇõrńęmaTkeraŠł∑abhŇębhŠĻõ
tńĀ yavanikńĀnńĀmnńĀ sudharmmńĀtmanńĀ tuŠĻáŠłćńęrńĀhvayamaŠĻáŠłćalńĀrhasu
girau yakŠĻ£eŇõvarau kalpitau .

div.p:
va√Īciyar kulapatiyeŠłĽiŠĻČi vakuttaviyakkariyakkiyaro
ŠĻ≠e√ĪciyavaŠłĽivu tiruttiyiveŠĻákuŠĻáaviŠĻüai tirumalai vaittńĀŠĻČ A
√ĪcitaŠĻČ vaŠłĽi varum vaŠĻČ vaŠłĽi mutali tali AtikaŠĻČavakaŠĻČ nŇęl vi√Īcaiyar
sthala puŠĻČai takamaiyar kńĀvalaŠĻČ viŠĻ≠ukńĀtaŠłĽakiyaypperumńĀŠł∑ey .

Hail ! Prosperity ! AtigaimńĀŠĻČ[i] of the Ňöera race placed on (the hill the images of) a yaksha and a yakshńę,‚ÄĒmeritorious gifts (formerly) made by EŠĻõiŠĻČi,‚ÄĒpresented a gong and gave a channel, which he had constructed (for feeding) the KaŠłćapperi (tank).

(The images of) the two lords of the yakshas, which were made on the excellent mountain of the Arha[t]Arha is probably a vulgar form for Arhat. in the country (maŠĻáŠłćala) called TuŠĻáŠłćńęra by the illustrious and pious king of KeraŠł∑a, called YavanikńĀ, were afterwards rescued from ruin by VyńĀmuktaŇõravaŠĻáojjvala, the lord of Taka[ŠĻ≠]ńĀ and son of the illustrious RńĀjarńĀja, an eminent prince, who was the ornament of his (YavanikńĀ's) race.

ViŠłću-kńĀdaŠĻõagiya-PerumńĀŠł∑ . . . . . . . . . . repaired the broken remains (of the images) of a yaksha and a yakshńę, which had been given by EŠĻõiŠĻČi, the lord of the race of the kings of Va√Īji, and placed them (on) this holy mountain (Tirumalai) of the god who possesses the eight qualities.EŠĻá-guŠĻáaŠĻČ is an attribute of an Arhat. The eight qualities are, according to the Jaina books: infinite wisdom, omniscience, omnipotence, boundless happiness, being nameless, without descent, without age and unobstructed. See Winslow, s. vv. eŠĻákuŠĻáaŠĻČ and eŠĻákuŠĻáam, pp. 172 and 320, and Dr. Graul's note on verse 9 of TiruvaŠł∑Šł∑uvar's KuŠĻüaŠł∑, p. 286.
Digital edition of SII 1.75 by converted to DHARMA conventions by Emmanuel Francis.
